# Sports Injury Prevention Program

## Injury Prevention Enhances Performance

Injury prevention programs have proven to be effective at reducing the risk of injuries involving the hip, knee and ankle in athletes across a wide range of sports. Youth athletes that participate in a simple warm-up with an injury prevention focus twice a week are 50% less likely to sustain a sports related injury.

At Wake Orthopaedics, we are proud to offer free injury prevention assessments to all local youth athletes at WakeMed Soccer Park and our Cary and Oberlin locations. All athletes will be sent home with a personalized injury prevention program that addresses their specific needs.

## FIFA 11+: A Warm-up Proven to Reduce Injuries

![sports warm up](/sites/default/files/inline-images/sports-injury-prevention1.jpg) Injury in an athlete is no small feat. It can lead to time away from sport and friends, unwanted visits to doctors and other medical professionals, and feelings of frustration.

![sports warm up](/sites/default/files/inline-images/sports-injury-prevention1.jpg)

At Wake Orthopaedics, we believe the best way to treat injuries is to prevent them through knowledge, education and implementation of a solid warm-up routine.

To keep you playing at the top of your game, injury free, we recommend following the FIFA 11+ program. Developed and studied by the FIFA Medical Assessment and Research Center (F-MARC), the FIFA 11+ is a 20-minute, warm-up program that when performed prior to training (at least twice a week) can reduce injuries by up to 50 percent while enhancing performance when proper technique is used.

# Athlete Injury Prevention Programs: Safeguarding Your Sporting Passion

![](https://www.coastsport.com.au/wp-content/uploads/2023/10/header-athlete-doing-strength-and-conditioning.png)

Returning to sport after an injury can be a daunting prospect, and athletes often wonder how to enhance their performance while minimising the risk of re-injury. Fortunately, many sporting codes have introduced specialised athletic preparation programs designed to address this concern.

In this guide, we explore various injury prevention programs tailored to popular sports played in Australia. From the well-known 11+ program designed by [FIFA](https://www.fifa.com/fifaplus/en) to the popular Dry Land Exercises for Swimmers by [Swimming Australia](https://www.swimming.org.au/), there are plenty of excellent programs out there that are bound to suit your sporting style.

![Coast Sport Desktop CTA](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%201456%20240'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![Coast Sport Desktop CTA](https://www.coastsport.com.au/wp-content/uploads/2018/12/CoastSport_blog-CTA_2x.webp)
![Coast Sport CTA](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20720%20480'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![Coast Sport CTA](https://www.coastsport.com.au/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/CoastSport_blog-CTA-mobile-1.webp)

## The Importance of Injury Prevention

Injury prevention stands as a cornerstone in the realm of athletic training, a fundamental principle that underpins every athlete’s journey. Its significance reverberates throughout an athlete’s career, from the novice to the seasoned professional, and is instrumental in the pursuit of excellence in sports.

If you’re an athlete reading this, we’re sure you understand all too well the pain, both physical and emotional, of sustaining a sport injury. While some injuries are relatively minimal and athletes can bounce back quickly, others can mean extensive recovery periods and even the end of a sporting career.

Aside from the obvious factor of continuing to play or compete in your sport, there are so many other important elements to injury prevention:

### Safeguarding Athletes’ Well-being

First and foremost, injury prevention serves as the guardian of athletes’ physical health. Sports, by their nature, push the human body to its limits. As an athlete, you’ll likely engage in rigorous training and high-impact activities and often face fierce competition.

As a result, you might expose yourself to various risks that can lead to injuries – from minor sprains to more severe, potentially career-altering conditions. Preventing these injuries is not merely a matter of enhancing performance but fundamentally about ensuring your physical and mental well-being as an athlete.

### Prolonged Athletic Careers

Injuries, particularly if they become recurrent or chronic, can cut short an athlete’s career. We see time and again so many promising athletes have their dreams shattered due to injuries that could have been prevented. It’s heart wrenching to see, and incredibly difficult for individuals to process and overcome.

By investing in injury prevention, athletes can prolong their sporting careers, giving themselves more time to evolve and excel in their chosen discipline. As the saying goes, “The best ability is availability.” Staying injury-free means athletes can consistently participate in their sport and continue to grow and thrive.

### Optimised Performance

This one’s obvious, yet it still bears mentioning. Injury prevention and performance enhancement are interconnected, more so than some may think.

Athletes who stay injury-free can commit to their training and competitions with more consistency. They can follow their training plan, not simply season-to-season, but over multiple seasons with years to steadily progress.

A lack of injuries ensures that athletes can undertake rigorous workouts and skill-building exercises without interruptions. This consistency is a vital driver of improved performance, as practice and effort translate into better skills, higher endurance, greater strength, and overall athletic excellence.

Another relevant factor here is age in sports. Many athletes will naturally reach the peak of their physical fitness and skill at a certain age depending on their sport, and oftentimes this peak is reached fairly young. Think of gymnasts, who can start to excel at international levels during teenage years.

Because of this timeframe, staying injury free is crucial to avoid delays in training and progression, allowing athletes to hit those peak performance brackets.

### Embracing Sporting Passion

Beyond the physical and performance-related aspects, injury prevention is intrinsically linked to the emotional and psychological well-being of athletes. The joy and passion for sports often drive athletes to pursue their goals tirelessly.

Injuries can erode this passion, leading to frustration, disappointment, and even disillusionment. By actively working to prevent injuries, athletes can continue to enjoy the sports they love, fostering a positive and enduring relationship with their chosen discipline.

![Injury prevention program for teams](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20720%20272'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![Injury prevention program for teams](https://www.coastsport.com.au/wp-content/uploads/2023/10/injury-prevention-program-for-teams.png)

## Key Components of Injury Prevention Programs

Injury prevention programs are multifaceted and well-rounded. They encompass various components that collectively reduce the risk of sport injuries.

While a good injury prevention program will ideally be tailored to your specific sport, and further customised to suit your individual training and performance style, you’ll likely find some of these core components in your routine:

### Warm-up Routines

Adequate warm-up routines are a fundamental starting point for any injury prevention program. A proper warm-up prepares the body for more strenuous activity by increasing heart rate, blood flow, and muscle temperature.

Athletes often engage in dynamic stretching and mobility exercises to enhance flexibility and joint range of motion. An effective warm-up readies the body for physical stress, making it less susceptible to injuries.

Proper warm-ups are important for all sports and all seasons, but even more so for [winter sports](https://www.coastsport.com.au/winter-sport-and-cold-weather-warm-up/). Cold weather can tighten the muscles and make tears, sprains, and other injuries much more likely.

### Pre-Activation Exercises

Pre-activation exercises involve engaging specific muscle groups that are critical for an athlete’s performance in their sport. These exercises prime the muscles, ensuring they are activated and ready to support the body during competition.

This component targets the muscles that are most vulnerable to injuries, such as the quadriceps and hamstrings in running sports or the shoulder and rotator cuff muscles in overhead sports like tennis or baseball.

### Strength and Conditioning Regimens

[Strength and conditioning](https://www.coastsport.com.au/what-is-strength-and-conditioning-and-how-can-it-benefit-your-fitness/) exercises are at the core of many injury prevention programs. These regimens help athletes build muscle strength, improve muscular endurance, and enhance joint stability.

Resistance training, plyometrics, and core strengthening exercises are commonly incorporated. Strengthening muscles and stabilising joints is vital for reducing the risk of various injuries, such as muscle strains, ligament sprains, and stress fractures.

### Sports-Specific Drills

Each sport has its unique set of movements and demands. Sports-specific drills replicate these movements, allowing athletes to practise and perfect their skills while minimising the risk of injuries.

For example, soccer players might focus on agility drills, while basketball players may work on their jumping and landing techniques. These drills help athletes develop muscle memory for safe and efficient movement patterns.

### Mental Conditioning

The mental aspect of injury prevention is often overlooked but equally important. Mental conditioning involves honing an athlete’s focus, concentration, and awareness. Athletes are taught to recognise when they might be pushing their bodies too hard or making errors that could lead to injuries.

Mental strength and resilience play a significant role in preventing overuse injuries, as athletes learn to listen to their bodies and make informed decisions during training and competitions.

### Nutritional Guidance

Nutrition is a critical aspect of injury prevention. A well-balanced diet provides the body with the essential nutrients it needs for optimal performance and recovery.

Proper nutrition helps in maintaining muscle strength and bone density, which are essential for preventing sports-related injuries. Seeing an accredited practicing [dietitian or nutritionist](https://www.coastsport.com.au/service/sports-nutrition/) can be extremely beneficial here.

### Rest and Recovery

Adequate rest and recovery are integral to injury prevention. Overtraining and insufficient recovery time can lead to fatigue, muscle imbalances, and compromised immunity, increasing the risk of injuries.

Injury prevention programs often include strategies for scheduling rest days and optimising recovery techniques, such as stretching, foam rolling, and of course, [adequate sleep](https://www.coastsport.com.au/sleep-for-recovery/).

### Regular Monitoring and Assessments

Injury prevention programs are not static; they evolve with an athlete’s needs and goals. Regular monitoring and assessments ensure that the program remains effective.

Periodic assessments can identify areas of improvement and track an athlete’s progress, making necessary adjustments to address vulnerabilities and enhance injury resilience.

## Benefits of Participating in Injury Prevention Programs

Engaging in injury prevention programs offers athletes a multitude of benefits. Beyond the reduced risk of injury, you can expect improvements in your physical performance. These improvements typically include increased agility, strength, endurance, and overall fitness.

By systematically incorporating these programs into your training routine, you can gain a competitive edge. The benefits of injury prevention extend beyond the field or court, contributing to your long-term success and well-being as an athlete.

It might be tempting to simply stick to your prescribed training regimen, which may even look somewhat similar to the core components above. When it comes to injuries, however, we can’t stress enough how important it is to incorporate some form of additional work into your normal routine in order to keep fit, strong, resilient, and injury-free.

## How to Implement Injury Prevention Strategies

Implementing injury prevention strategies involves a systematic approach that should be guided by your coach, psychotherapist, or exercise physiologist.

Successful execution of these strategies requires the integration of specific exercises, drills, and mental conditioning techniques into an athlete’s regular training regimen. These exercises should be tailored to address the unique demands of the sport.

Athletes and coaches must collaborate to create a comprehensive injury prevention program. This program should encompass warm-up routines, skill development, and mental conditioning to prepare athletes both physically and mentally for the challenges of their chosen discipline.

![Injury prevention exercise at Coast Sport](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20720%20272'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![Injury prevention exercise at Coast Sport](https://www.coastsport.com.au/wp-content/uploads/2023/10/injury-prevention-exercise.png)

## Choosing the Right Injury Prevention Program for Your Sport

Different sports have distinct injury patterns and risk factors. Netball, for example, is renowned for [knee](https://www.coastsport.com.au/common-netball-injuries-and-prevention/) and ACL injuries. High-contact sports like AFL, rugby, and soccer see plenty of [concussions](https://www.coastsport.com.au/how-to-recognise-and-treat-concussions-in-sport/). Swimmers can also be prone to injuries, such as (surprisingly) lower [limb injuries in breaststroke swimmers](https://www.coastsport.com.au/lower-limb-injuries-in-breaststroke-swimmers/)https://www.coastsport.com.au/conditions/acl-injury/.

Specific injuries prevention programs have been developed to address the unique needs of athletes in each discipline, and it’s here that you’ll really see a difference when choosing the appropriate program for your sport.

Some of the most notable and highly-regarded injury prevention programs include:

**Swimming Australia Dry Land Exercises (Swimming):** Swimming Australia’s [dry land exercise program](https://wa.swimming.org.au/sites/default/files/assets/documents/stretching%20poster%20A1print.pdf) is designed for swimmers aged 10 and above. It comprises a series of exercises that serve as a warm-up for swimming training, both physically and mentally. These exercises are essential for improving swimmers’ performance and reducing the risk of injuries.

![Athletes at the Coast Sport reception desk](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20720%20272'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![Athletes at the Coast Sport reception desk](https://www.coastsport.com.au/wp-content/uploads/2023/10/athletes-at-the-coast-sport-reception-desk.png)

## Arm Yourself Against Injuries With Coast Sport

Sport injuries are far too common, and at Coast Sport, they make up a huge portion of our clients’ problems. Our practitioners are highly experienced in designing and implementing injury prevention and performance enhancement programs.

Whether you’re an aspiring athlete looking to minimise your injury risk or a seasoned player aiming to boost your performance, our programs are tailored to meet your specific needs. Safeguard your passion for sports by staying injury-free, achieving your athletic goals, and enjoying a long and successful sporting career.   
[Contact us](https://www.coastsport.com.au/contact-us/) today to begin your journey towards excellence in sports while reducing the risk of sport injuries!

SUMMARY:
We searched the PubMed, CINAHL, EMBASE, and the Cochrane databases for systematic reviews and meta-analyses that evaluated the effectiveness of sports injury prevention programs. The purpose of this comprehensive review was to quantify the number of systematic reviews and meta-analyses that have examined the effectiveness of sports injury prevention programs on reducing musculoskeletal injuries, evaluate the quality of each systematic review, identify the primary studies included in each review, and provide a succinct summary of this vast body of literature for easy reference. We included systematic reviews and meta-analyses that evaluated the effectiveness of sports injury prevention programs on reducing musculoskeletal injuries. Abstract
--------

### Background:

A large volume of systematic reviews and meta-analyses has been published on the effectiveness of sports injury prevention programs.

### Purpose:

To provide a qualitative summary of published systematic reviews and meta-analyses that have examined the effectiveness of sports injury prevention programs on reducing musculoskeletal injuries.

### Study Design:

Systematic review; Level of evidence, 4.

### Methods:

We searched the PubMed, CINAHL, EMBASE, and the Cochrane databases for systematic reviews and meta-analyses that evaluated the effectiveness of sports injury prevention programs. We excluded published abstracts, narrative reviews, articles not published in English, commentaries, studies that described sports injury prevention strategies but did not assess their effectiveness, studies that did not assess musculoskeletal injuries, and studies that did not assess sports-related injuries. The most relevant results were extracted and summarized. Levels of evidence were determined per the Oxford Centre for Evidence-Based Medicine, and methodological quality was assessed using the AMSTAR-2 (A MeaSurement Tool to Assess systematic Reviews, revised version).

### Results:

A total of 507 articles were retrieved, and 129 were included. Articles pertaining to all injuries were divided into 9 topics: sports and exercise in general (n = 20), soccer (n = 13), ice hockey (n = 1), dance (n = 1), volleyball (n = 1), basketball (n = 1), tackle collision sports (n = 1), climbing (n = 1), and youth athletes (n = 4). Articles on injuries by anatomic site were divided into 11 topics: general knee (n = 8), anterior cruciate ligament (n = 34), ankle (n = 14), hamstring (n = 11), lower extremity (n = 10), foot (n = 6), groin (n = 2), shoulder (n = 1), wrist (n = 2), and elbow (n = 1). Of the 129 studies, 45.7% were ranked as evidence level 1, and 55.0% were evidence level 2. Based on the AMSTAR-2, 58.9% of the reviews reported a priori review methods, 96.1% performed a comprehensive literature search, 47.3% thoroughly described excluded articles, 79.1% assessed risk of bias for individual studies, 48.8% reported a valid method for statistical combination of data (ie, meta-analysis), 45.0% examined the effect of risk of bias on pooled study results, and 19.4% examined the risk for publication bias.

### Conclusion:

This comprehensive review provides sports medicine providers with a single source of the most up-to-date publications in the literature on sports injury prevention.

**Keywords:** sports injuries, prevention, musculoskeletal, systematic review, meta-analysis

* * *

In the United States, approximately 4.3 million nonfatal sports or recreation-related injuries are seen annually in the emergency department.[81](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr81-23259671211035776) The highest rates of sports injuries for both boys and girls occur in adolescents aged 10 to 14 years, which is likely due to increased participation in sports among this age group.[81](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr81-23259671211035776) The lower extremity is most commonly injured during sports participation; however, the incidence of injury to specific body parts varies by sport.[81](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr81-23259671211035776),[185](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr185-23259671211035776),[257](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr257-23259671211035776) Owing to the influx of participants in sports and the subsequent injuries they sustain, research on sports injury prevention strategies is rapidly growing.[129](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr129-23259671211035776) Injury prevention is important for reducing long-term health consequences, such as disability, and minimizing the economic burden of treatment.[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr2-23259671211035776)

Injury prevention strategies typically focus on modifiable risk factors, such as rules, equipment, and physical fitness, and prevention strategies may be tailored to a specific sport or injury.[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr2-23259671211035776) Exercise training involves learning proper exercise techniques, such as understanding the limits of range of motion of each joint and avoiding joint positions that place the anatomic structures of the joint at risk for injury.[40](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr40-23259671211035776) Exercise training usually involves some combination of strength, proprioceptive, balance, and neuromuscular training that is important for improving athletic performance and preventing injuries.[30](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr30-23259671211035776),[107](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr107-23259671211035776),[127](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr127-23259671211035776)

The purpose of this comprehensive review was to quantify the number of systematic reviews and meta-analyses that have examined the effectiveness of sports injury prevention programs on reducing musculoskeletal injuries, evaluate the quality of each systematic review, identify the primary studies included in each review, and provide a succinct summary of this vast body of literature for easy reference. We hypothesized that the largest number of articles would (1) pertain to sports injury prevention in general (as opposed to prevention strategies for specific sports) and (2) focus on preventing knee injuries that are common in sports.

Methods
-------

We performed a literature search in accordance with PRISMA (Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses)[151](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr151-23259671211035776) using PubMed, CINAHL, EMBASE, and the Cochrane databases to identify articles published in English between inception of these databases and August 31, 2020. Additional articles were found by hand searching the reference lists of included articles. The search terms were (“prevention” or “prevention programs”) AND (“sports injury” OR “musculoskeletal injury”) AND (“systematic review” or “meta-analysis”). We included systematic reviews and meta-analyses that evaluated the effectiveness of sports injury prevention programs on reducing musculoskeletal injuries. We excluded published abstracts, narrative reviews, articles not published in English, commentaries, studies that described sports injury prevention strategies but did not assess the effectiveness of these strategies, studies that did not assess musculoskeletal injuries, and studies that did not assess sports-related injuries.

The results of the entire literature search were entered into a single database, and duplicates were removed via electronic search and double-checked manually. Two of the authors (S.D.S. and M.A.K.) independently screened the results of the literature search, and 3 authors (S.D.S., J.W.K., and A.V.V.) reviewed studies that met inclusion in more detail and summarized the germane results. The articles that met inclusion criteria were summarized by 2 major topics: (1) all injuries and (2) injuries by anatomic site. Articles pertaining to all injuries were further subdivided into 9 topics: sports and exercise in general, soccer, ice hockey, dance, volleyball, basketball, tackle collision sports, climbing, and youth athletes. Articles pertaining to injuries by anatomic site were subdivided into 11 topics: general knee, anterior cruciate ligament (ACL), ankle, hamstring, lower extremity, foot, groin, shoulder, wrist, shin, and elbow. We determined the level of evidence for each systematic review according to the definitions set forth by the Oxford Centre for Evidence-Based Medicine.[88](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr88-23259671211035776) Methodological quality was assessed using the AMSTAR-2 (A MeaSurement Tool to Assess systematic Reviews, revised version).[205](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr205-23259671211035776) The list of 129 articles was divided in half among 4 authors (S.D.S., M.A.K., J.W.K., and A.V.V.) for AMSTAR-2 assessment, and thus each study was assessed independently by 2 authors (S.D.S. and M.A.K.). Last, we tallied the number of overlapping primary studies between systematic reviews within a particular topic.

Results
-------

[Figure 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#fig1-23259671211035776) presents the PRISMA (Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses) flowchart illustrating the literature search process. The literature search identified 507 unique articles, and of these, 129 met the inclusion criteria. [Table 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#table1-23259671211035776) illustrates the number of included articles by topic. The majority of “all injury” articles pertained to sports and exercise in general (15.5%) and soccer (10.1%). Most of the “injuries by anatomic site” articles pertained to ACL (26.4%), ankle (10.9%), and hamstring (8.5%) injuries. Three of the 129 included articles covered >1 anatomic site. The level of evidence and the AMSTAR-2 results are shown in Table S1 (available as Supplemental Material). A total of 59 (45.7%) of the included systematic reviews were ranked as evidence level 1, and 71 (55.0%) were level 2. Based on the AMSTAR-2, 76 (58.9%) reviews reported a priori review methods, 124 (96.1%) performed a comprehensive literature search, 61 (47.3%) thoroughly described excluded articles, 102 (79.1%) assessed risk of bias for individual studies, 63 (48.8%) reported a valid method for statistical combination of data (ie, meta-analysis), 58 (45.0%) examined the effect of risk of bias on pooled study results, and 25 (19.4%) examined the risk for publication bias.

### All Injuries

A total of 43 studies pertaining to prevention of all injuries were identified and are summarized by topic here. Tables S2 to S4 and Figures S1 to S3 (available as Supplemental Material) illustrate the overlapping primary studies between included systematic reviews.

#### Sports and Exercise in General

A number of overlapping systematic reviews[‡](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#fn1-23259671211035776) have examined the effectiveness of various injury prevention strategies for sports and exercise in general. Salam et al[194](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr194-23259671211035776) pooled data from 24 studies and found that sports injury prevention interventions were effective in that interventions decreased the incidence of (1) injuries in general (relative risk [RR], 0.66; 95% confidence interval [CI], 0.53-0.82), (2) injuries per hour of exposure (RR, 0.63; 95% CI, 0.47-0.86), and (3) injuries per number of exposures (RR, 0.79; 95% CI, 0.70-0.88); however, the results of their study were not stratified by type of intervention. Two meta-analyses by Lauersen et al[127](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr127-23259671211035776),[128](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr128-23259671211035776) found that strength training was effective for reducing the risk of sports injuries, and 1 of these studies[128](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr128-23259671211035776) also found that proprioception training (RR, 0.55; 95% CI, 0.35-0.87) and a combination of exercises (RR, 0.66; 95% CI, 0.52-0.83) were effective at reducing sports injuries. Smyth et al[214](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr214-23259671211035776) found moderate evidence demonstrating the effectiveness of exercise and psychological interventions for reducing injuries in pre-elite athletes competing in the Olympics or professional sports, but evidence was lacking with respect to the effectiveness of equipment and nutrition-related interventions. Two systematic reviews[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr1-23259671211035776),[129](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr129-23259671211035776) found that the use of insoles, external joint supports, and multi-intervention or sport-specific training programs were effective for injury prevention. Of note, several overlapping systematic reviews have found no or minimal evidence regarding the effectiveness of stretching,[§](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#fn2-23259671211035776) warm-up exercises,[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr1-23259671211035776),[69](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr69-23259671211035776) or prevention videos[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr1-23259671211035776),[129](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr129-23259671211035776) for injury prevention. Two systematic reviews[30](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr30-23259671211035776),[107](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr107-23259671211035776) demonstrated that balance training was favorable for reducing overall injuries (RR, 0.49; 95% CI, 0.13-1.80) and defined the optimal balance training program for injury prevention as lasting for 8 weeks and including two 45-minute training sessions per week. Three overlapping systematic reviews[77](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr77-23259671211035776),[131](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr131-23259671211035776),[239](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr239-23259671211035776) found that psychological interventions, including cognitive behavioral therapy and stress management—based interventions, were effective at reducing sports injuries. Ernst and Posadzki[60](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr60-23259671211035776) found conflicting results regarding the effectiveness of chiropractic interventions for preventing sports injuries in their review of 6 studies. To summarize, there are multiple systematic reviews and meta-analyses demonstrating that injury prevention programs are effective, with strength, proprioception, balance, and psychological programs being specifically beneficial.

#### Soccer

Four overlapping systematic reviews[43](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr43-23259671211035776),[64](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr64-23259671211035776),[182](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr182-23259671211035776),[243](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr243-23259671211035776) found mixed results for exercise-based prevention programs in soccer players, and all of these reviews were poor quality and at high risk for bias. Based on their review of 18 studies, Kirkendall et al[123](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr123-23259671211035776) concluded that a structured warm-up program can be effective at reducing soccer injuries by one-third. Based on their review of 13 studies, Slimani et al[211](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr211-23259671211035776) found that psychological-based interventions were effective at reducing injury rates in soccer players. Seven overlapping systematic reviews and meta-analyses[4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr4-23259671211035776),[6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr6-23259671211035776),[21](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr21-23259671211035776),[79](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr79-23259671211035776),[119](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr119-23259671211035776),[192](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr192-23259671211035776),[238](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr238-23259671211035776) examined the effectiveness of 2 soccer injury prevention programs developed by the Federation Internationale de Football Association (FIFA). The FIFA 11 and FIFA 11+ programs combine training in both technique and balance along with neuromuscular training exercises including strengthening and plyometrics with the goal of eliminating injuries. Al Attar et al[4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr4-23259671211035776) examined the combined effects of FIFA 11 and FIFA 11+ and found that overall injuries were reduced by 34% (RR, 0.66; 95% CI, 0.60 to 0.73) and lower limb injuries were reduced by 29% (RR, 0.71; 95% CI, 0.63 to 0.81). Gomes Neto et al[79](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr79-23259671211035776) observed that FIFA 11 was associated with reduced injury risk (RR, 0.69; 95% CI, 0.49 to 0.98) and improvement in both dynamic balance (weighted mean difference, 2.68; 95% CI, 0.44 to 4.92) and agility (standardized mean difference, –0.36; 95% CI, –0.70 to 0.02) compared with controls. Two systematic reviews[21](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr21-23259671211035776),[192](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr192-23259671211035776) demonstrated that FIFA 11+ resulted in (1) 30% to 70% fewer injuries and (2) significant improvement in many features of motor and neuromuscular performance. Two systematic reviews[6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr6-23259671211035776),[238](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr238-23259671211035776) directly compared FIFA 11 and FIFA 11+ and found that FIFA 11+ was associated with a greater reduction in injuries. In their review of 33 articles, Kilic et al[119](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr119-23259671211035776) concluded that the FIFA 11+ program and the Nordic Hamstring Exercise (NHE) program were most effective for reducing musculoskeletal injuries in adult soccer players, while FIFA 11, balance board, the Prevent Injury and Enhance Performance program, a groin program, and balance board training were not effective injury prevention programs. To summarize, the FIFA 11+ program has been shown to reduce injuries and improve neuromuscular performance in soccer.

#### Ice Hockey

Cusimano et al[45](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr45-23259671211035776) identified 13 studies that examined rule changes intended to minimize aggression (eg, restriction of body-checking) during ice hockey, of which 9 studies demonstrated a decrease in penalties ranging from 1.2 to 5.9 per game and in injury rates by 3- to 12-fold.

#### Dance

Hincapie et al[103](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr103-23259671211035776) reviewed 2 cohort studies and concluded that there was weak evidence for the effectiveness of injury prevention programs in dancers.

#### Volleyball

Kilic et al[120](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr120-23259671211035776) found evidence from 3 studies that (1) a resistance training program, (2) a program aimed at reducing anterior knee pain, and (3) balance board training were effective at reducing musculoskeletal injuries in volleyball players.

#### Basketball

Based on their systematic review of 4 studies, Kilic et al[121](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr121-23259671211035776) concluded that each of the following programs was successful at reducing musculoskeletal injuries in basketball players: (1) a jump-landing technique intervention during warm-up, (2) a multistation proprioceptive exercise program, (3) a wobble board and sport-specific balance training program during warm-up, and (4) the FIFA 11+ program.

#### Tackle Collision Sports

Sewry et al[202](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr202-23259671211035776) identified 7 studies that demonstrated that exercise-based intervention programs were successful at reducing injuries in tackle collision sports (ie, rugby union or league and American, Australian, and Gaelic football).

#### Climbing

Based on their systematic review of 19 studies, Woollings et al[260](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr260-23259671211035776) found that both taping and weight training reduced injury rates in sport climbing and bouldering. However, the authors did not find any support for stretching, yoga, using instructors, regulating equipment usage, taking supplements, heating hands before climbing, and corticosteroid injections as injury prevention measures for sport climbing.

#### Youth Athletes

Several overlapping systematic reviews[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr2-23259671211035776),[37](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr37-23259671211035776),[189](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr189-23259671211035776),[217](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr217-23259671211035776) have examined the effectiveness of various sports injury prevention strategies in youth (ie, adolescents or teenagers aged <20 years). Soomro et al[217](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr217-23259671211035776) pooled data from 10 studies and found a 40% reduction (injury rate ratio, 0.60; 95% CI, 0.48-0.75) in injuries for multifaceted injury prevention programs (ie, a combination of warm-up, neuromuscular strength, and proprioception training) in adolescent team sports. Abernethy and Bleakley[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8558815/#bibr2-23259671211035776) reviewed 12 studies and found preseason conditioning, functional training, education, balance, and sport-specific skills to be moderately effective at preventing adolescent sports injuries. SUMMARY:
There is however, the significant burden of sport-related musculoskeletal injury, with the greatest risk being in the youth and young adult populations. [1] It is vital to incorporate primary injury prevention and make this a public health priority as this will have significant implications for reducing long-term consequences of musculoskeletal injuries, such as early post-traumatic osteoarthritis. The sport-related injury burden is however significant and there is a need for research into the evaluation of injury prevention strategies in all sports across all ages. [6] From these injury rates and high financial costs, it is clear that the injury-burden is significant and that there is a need to implement evidence-based injury prevention strategies to reduce the risk of injury in youth, and also across the lifespan.

Introduction[[edit](https://www.physio-pedia.com/index.php?title=Injury_Prevention_in_Sport&veaction=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Introduction") | [edit source](https://www.physio-pedia.com/index.php?title=Injury_Prevention_in_Sport&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Introduction")]
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[Physical activity](https://www.physio-pedia.com/Physical_Activity "Physical Activity") and sports participation is encouraged by all health care professionals as it has numerous positive effects on a person's health. There is however, the significant burden of sport-related musculoskeletal injury, with the greatest risk being in the youth and young adult populations.[[1]](https://www.physio-pedia.com/Injury_Prevention_in_Sport#cite_note-:0-1) It is vital to incorporate primary injury prevention and make this a public health priority as this will have significant implications for reducing long-term consequences of musculoskeletal injuries, such as early post-traumatic [osteoarthritis](https://www.physio-pedia.com/Osteoarthritis "Osteoarthritis").[[1]](https://www.physio-pedia.com/Injury_Prevention_in_Sport#cite_note-:0-1)

The Importance of Sport Injury Prevention[[edit](https://www.physio-pedia.com/index.php?title=Injury_Prevention_in_Sport&veaction=edit&section=2 "Edit section: The Importance of Sport Injury Prevention") | [edit source](https://www.physio-pedia.com/index.php?title=Injury_Prevention_in_Sport&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: The Importance of Sport Injury Prevention")]
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

One of the building blocks of a healthy lifestyle across the lifespan is [physical activity](https://www.physio-pedia.com/Physical_Activity "Physical Activity") and participation in sport and recreation is encouraged by all health care professionals. The sport-related injury burden is however significant and there is a need for research into the evaluation of injury prevention strategies in all sports across all ages.[[2]](https://www.physio-pedia.com/Injury_Prevention_in_Sport#cite_note-:1-2) The youth and young adult populations have the highest participation rates, but also the highest injury rates and sport is the leading cause of injury in youth.[[3]](https://www.physio-pedia.com/Injury_Prevention_in_Sport#cite_note-3)[[4]](https://www.physio-pedia.com/Injury_Prevention_in_Sport#cite_note-4) Studies have shown that 20% of schoolchildren will miss at least one day of school per year due to sports injuries, and one in three youth seek medical attention for sports-related injuries per year. Even adults lose at least one day a year from work as a result of sport-related injury.[[2]](https://www.physio-pedia.com/Injury_Prevention_in_Sport#cite_note-:1-2)[[5]](https://www.physio-pedia.com/Injury_Prevention_in_Sport#cite_note-5) Sport is the the leading cause of all injuries in youth, but also has an impact on the adult population. Furthermore, the financial implications of sport-related injuries are huge. In Australia alone the direct cost of sport-related injury over a seven year period amounted to an estimated 265 million Australian dollars.[[6]](https://www.physio-pedia.com/Injury_Prevention_in_Sport#cite_note-6) From these injury rates and high financial costs, it is clear that the injury-burden is significant and that there is a need to implement evidence-based injury prevention strategies to reduce the risk of injury in youth, and also across the lifespan. Lower extremity injuries are the highest overall burden of sport-related injury at 60%, of which 60% of these are ankle and knee joint injuries.[[1]](https://www.physio-pedia.com/Injury_Prevention_in_Sport#cite_note-:0-1)

Injuries in sport may also contribute to the rising burden of overweight and [obesity](https://www.physio-pedia.com/Obesity "Obesity") in youth, with 8% of youth dropping out of sport per year because of injury or fear of injury.[[7]](https://www.physio-pedia.com/Injury_Prevention_in_Sport#cite_note-7)[[8]](https://www.physio-pedia.com/Injury_Prevention_in_Sport#cite_note-8) This leads to a further decline in physical activity participation and this has negative implications (obesity, post-traumatic [osteoarthritis](https://www.physio-pedia.com/Osteoarthritis "Osteoarthritis")) on future health. Reducing the significant burden of sport-related injury would have great impact on quality of life through the promotion of physical activity.[[1]](https://www.physio-pedia.com/Injury_Prevention_in_Sport#cite_note-:0-1)

Physical activity, sport and recreation is vital in youth and for all age groups to have a healthy lifestyle, to promote healthy growth and development, to prevent chronic disease and to reduce stress.These benefits from participation in sport and recreation has important implications for public health, but the injury risk must be balanced and addressed.[[1]](https://www.physio-pedia.com/Injury_Prevention_in_Sport#cite_note-:0-1)

Injury Prevention - A Systematic Approach[[edit](https://www.physio-pedia.com/index.php?title=Injury_Prevention_in_Sport&veaction=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Injury Prevention - A Systematic Approach") | [edit source](https://www.physio-pedia.com/index.php?title=Injury_Prevention_in_Sport&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Injury Prevention - A Systematic Approach")]
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[![Image 12](https://www.physio-pedia.com/images/thumb/b/b4/Van_Mechelen_Injury_Prevention_Model.png/250px-Van_Mechelen_Injury_Prevention_Model.png)](https://www.physio-pedia.com/File:Van_Mechelen_Injury_Prevention_Model.png)

[](https://www.physio-pedia.com/File:Van_Mechelen_Injury_Prevention_Model.png "Enlarge")

Injury Prevention Model as described by Van Mechelen[[9]](https://www.physio-pedia.com/Injury_Prevention_in_Sport#cite_note-9)

Van Mechelen (1992) proposed and developed the four-step model for injury prevention in sports and this model has been the foundation for the development and evaluation of injury prevention programs since it inception.[[10]](https://www.physio-pedia.com/Injury_Prevention_in_Sport#cite_note-10) The model includes the following steps that may be followed in the prevention of sports injuries:

*   Establishing the extent of injury in a specific population through surveillance systems
*   Identifying the risk factors for injury in a specific population
*   Development and validation of injury prevention strategies
*   Evaluation of these injury prevention strategies by measuring the impact of the prevention strategy on the incidence of injuries using appropriate surveillance systems

Various study designs are used to investigate the effectiveness of injury prevention strategies, and although randomised controlled trials are the golden standard, it is not always possible or ethical, therefore less rigorous study designs such as quasi-experimental, cohort and case-control studies are also used. As a result there are usually inherent methodological limitations that do introduce bias and thus limits the interpretation of study findings to some extent.[[11]](https://www.physio-pedia.com/Injury_Prevention_in_Sport#cite_note-11)[[12]](https://www.physio-pedia.com/Injury_Prevention_in_Sport#cite_note-12)

Emery and Pasanen (2019) states that: _"The "best" injury prevention program is the one that can and will be adopted and sustained by athletes, coaches, and sporting bodies."_[[1]](https://www.physio-pedia.com/Injury_Prevention_in_Sport#cite_note-:0-1) Injury prevention research has gained ground and is being used in real world settings. The challenge being to implement cost effective injury prevention strategies in real life circumstances.[[13]](https://www.physio-pedia.com/Injury_Prevention_in_Sport#cite_note-13) One of the projects that addresses this challenge is the Translating Research into the Injury Prevention Practice (TRIPP) Framework.[[14]](https://www.physio-pedia.com/Injury_Prevention_in_Sport#cite_note-:2-14) The TRIPP framework is an extension of the Van Mechelen model and includes two additional steps that are necessary for the translation of the effectiveness of injury prevention strategies into real life practice.[[14]](https://www.physio-pedia.com/Injury_Prevention_in_Sport#cite_note-:2-14) The two additional steps include the understanding of the real world for which the specific intervention is being developed and the evaluation of this intervention in a real-world setting. Factors that should be considered when determining if an injury prevention program will be feasible in the real world may include:[[14]](https://www.physio-pedia.com/Injury_Prevention_in_Sport#cite_note-:2-14)

*   the sport participants' age group
*   the level of play
*   type of sport
*   organisational structure in which original intervention was evaluated.

[![Image 13](https://www.physio-pedia.com/images/thumb/d/d5/Translating_research_into_injury_prevention_practice_%28TRIPP%29.jpg/300px-Translating_research_into_injury_prevention_practice_%28TRIPP%29.jpg)](https://www.physio-pedia.com/File:Translating_research_into_injury_prevention_practice_(TRIPP).jpg)

[](https://www.physio-pedia.com/File:Translating_research_into_injury_prevention_practice_(TRIPP).jpg "Enlarge")

Translating research into injury prevention practice (TRIPP) as described by Finch.

Another framework that has received increased attention in the context of sports injury prevention strategies is the Reach Efficiency Adoption Implementation Maintenance (RE-AIM) Framework. It was originally developed to investigate the impact of health promotion interventions. It describes five steps to translate research into action:[[15]](https://www.physio-pedia.com/Injury_Prevention_in_Sport#cite_note-15)

*   reach the target population
*   effectiveness or efficacy
*   adoption by target audience
*   implementation consistency and costs and adaptations made during delivery
*   maintenance of intervention effects in individuals and over time

Multiple factors influence the adherence to an the implementation strategy that will maximise the effectiveness of a specific sport injury prevention program. It is necessary to create a good balance between evidence and real world implementation throughout the development and evaluation of the intervention, to alleviate problems of poor adherence.[[1]](https://www.physio-pedia.com/Injury_Prevention_in_Sport#cite_note-:0-1) Owoeye et al (2018) described the four key steps towards a more rigorous approach that may promote adherence to interventions.[[16]](https://www.physio-pedia.com/Injury_Prevention_in_Sport#cite_note-16)

[![Image 14](https://www.physio-pedia.com/images/thumb/9/90/Promotion_of_adherence_to_interventions.jpg/300px-Promotion_of_adherence_to_interventions.jpg)](https://www.physio-pedia.com/File:Promotion_of_adherence_to_interventions.jpg)

[](https://www.physio-pedia.com/File:Promotion_of_adherence_to_interventions.jpg "Enlarge")

The four key steps toward more rigorous approach to promote adherence to interventions as described by Owoeye et al.[[17]](https://www.physio-pedia.com/Injury_Prevention_in_Sport#cite_note-17)

Sport Injury Prevention Programs[[edit](https://www.physio-pedia.com/index.php?title=Injury_Prevention_in_Sport&veaction=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Sport Injury Prevention Programs") | [edit source](https://www.physio-pedia.com/index.php?title=Injury_Prevention_in_Sport&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Sport Injury Prevention Programs")]
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Sport injury prevention programs can reduce the number of injuries as well as the severity and extent of injuries.[[18]](https://www.physio-pedia.com/Injury_Prevention_in_Sport#cite_note-:3-18) The three areas where injury prevention in sport is focused on is:

*   training strategies
*   modification of sport rules and changes in policies
*   equipment recommendations

Training strategies focus on modifiable intrinsic (athlete related) risk factors such as strength, endurance and balance through neuromuscular exercise interventions. Extrinsic (environmental) risk factors are targeted through rule modification and equipment strategies (e.g. body checking in youth ice hockey, ankle braces, cricketers wearing British Standard compliant helmets when batting, wicket keeping or fielding close to the batter.)

### Neuromuscular training injury prevention programs[[edit](https://www.physio-pedia.com/index.php?title=Injury_Prevention_in_Sport&veaction=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Neuromuscular training injury prevention programs") | [edit source](https://www.physio-pedia.com/index.php?title=Injury_Prevention_in_Sport&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Neuromuscular training injury prevention programs")]

Prospective studies often investigate neuromuscular training injury prevention programs in team sports with attention toward amateur and elite athletes (adult and youth). These programs are often led by the coach or trainer, after a comprehensive training workshop by a physiotherapist or strength and conditioning coach with the relevant expertise.[[1]](https://www.physio-pedia.com/Injury_Prevention_in_Sport#cite_note-:0-1)

Neuromuscular injury prevention programs do reduce the risk of injury as seen in various studies.[[18]](https://www.physio-pedia.com/Injury_Prevention_in_Sport#cite_note-:3-18)[[19]](https://www.physio-pedia.com/I

enriched_points.expanded_content: Injury prevention programs, like FIFA 11+, have proven effective, reducing injuries by up to 50% across various sports, particularly in youth athletes.

enriched_points.point_title: Components of Effective Injury Prevention Programs
enriched_points.expanded_content: Successful programs integrate warm-up routines, strength training, sports-specific drills, and psychological conditioning, addressing both physical and mental preparedness for athletes.
Technology, including wearable devices and mobile apps, enhances monitoring of athletes' performance, tracks injury risks, and provides data for tailored training adjustments.
Numerous studies highlight the effectiveness of targeted injury prevention programs, with significant reductions in knee and ankle injuries among youth and professional athletes.
Future strategies will focus on personalized injury prevention plans, enhanced technology integration, and increased emphasis on mental health and wellness for athletes.
